frame_label: Glorification of Mother's Sacrifices / Mom's Home Cooking
generated_comment: Kya khoob tha woh zamana jab Ammi subah uthkar taaza garam nashta banati thi. Humare liye kitni mehnat karti thi, kabhi shikayat nahi ki. Us 'Maa ke haath ke khane' jaisa swaad aur pyaar, aajkal kahan milta hai? Ab toh sab Maggi aur bahar ke packets hai. Woh asliyat, woh pyaar sirf maa ke haath mein tha.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment glorifies the mother's domestic labor (cooking, waking up early, working hard) as an act of pure love, implicitly erasing it as 'work' and sentimentalizing her sacrifice. It then sets this standard by unfavorably comparing modern convenience food (Maggi, packaged food) to 'Maa ke haath ka khana,' subtly implying that current domestic providers (wives, daughters-in-law) are deficient if they don't match this idealized, tireless service.
original_frame: Religious and Caste Identity as Dress Mandate: Her Body as Community Property
persona_text: You are Ali. Age 48. Married. A Muslim man in Hyderabad. Your 20 year old daughter Sana has started college this year. She has been wearing a hijab since she was 16 Ñ your family is conservative and this has always been understood. Last week you found out from your wife that Sana has been removing her hijab once she reaches college and putting it back on before coming home. She does not know you know. You are sitting with your wife tonight deciding what to say to Sana tomorrow and what this behaviour means and what the consequences should be and what a Muslim girl who removes her hijab at the college gate is telling her family and her community about who she is choosing to be.
